summary: Patrick McKenzie reports that his side business Bingo Card Creator earned $2,925 in February 2009, surpassing his monthly salary of $2,890 as a Japanese salaryman. He tempers the milestone by noting that Japanese salaries include biannual bonuses and that self-employment would increase his tax burden. He also reveals that BCC had $995 in expenses that month. The post hints at a dramatic difference in hours worked between his day job and his side business.
key_points: BCC monthly revenue ($2,925) exceeded his monthly Japanese salary ($2,890) for the first time
The comparison is less dramatic when accounting for biannual bonuses, expenses, and tax differences
Japanese programmers in their mid-to-late twenties earn roughly $40k USD, which is considered good locally but far below US engineering salaries
The hours-to-income ratio between his day job and side business is dramatically different in favor of BCC
money_quotes: This brings BCC sales for February to $2,925.
I won't tell you how many hours are required to get either of these numbers… principally because the difference is too depressing for words.
The total salary of a programmer in his mid/late twenties in Japan is generally somewhere around the 4 million yen mark (~$40k USD). It is considered fairly good for someone that age.
We're crazy underpaid next to engineers in America, I know, I know — my family reminds me frequently.
key_insight: When a side project's monthly revenue surpasses your salary despite requiring far fewer hours, it forces a serious reckoning about the value of traditional employment.
